coredata.dc:description: Anion exchange membranes (AEMs) that enable effective mono-/divalent anion separation are highly desired for many environment-related applications. Conventional AEMs are characterized with microphase-separated microstructures and rely on the electrostatic attraction between anions and positively charged elements within hydrophilic regions to selectively transport anions. Sufficient charged groups are usually required to ensure high anion permeability, but the stronger interaction encountered by divalent anions than that by monovalent anions results in very low selectivity. We here propose a shielding effect-based strategy, that is, simply mixing piperidinium-based AEMs with carboxylated graphene quantum dots (GQD). These negatively charged GQD can electrostatically interact with the polymer matrix, thereby enhancing the connectivity of hydrophilic regions and also leading to an obvious shielding effect which causes a larger decrease in electrostatic attraction for divalent anions than that for monovalent anions. With the introduction of GQD, the resulting AEMs show obvious enhancement in both anion permeability and anion selectivity, with a Cl− transport rate of 3.2 mol m−2 h−1 and a Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity of 21.8 (8 times of the commercial ACS, 5 times of the original AEMs without GQD), and exceed other reported microphase-separated polymer membranes. This work offers a simple and promising strategy for high-performance ion-selective membranes.

(b) HRTEM image of the GQD (Inset: the size distribution histogram of the GQD). (c) The digital photograph of a transparent QP + GQD-5% membrane with an area of 30 × 30 cm2. (d) The mechanical strength of the QP, QP + GQD-1%, QP + GQD-5%, and QP + GQD-10 % membranes. (e) FTIR spectra of the GQD, QP, and QP + GQD membranes. (f) The surface SEM image of the QP + GQD-5% membrane (The insert is an elemental mapping of Na of the surface). (g) The cross-sectional SEM image of the QP + GQD-5% membrane. (The insert is an elemental mapping of Na of the cross-section).

(a) The ESP distribution of the piperidinium structural unit and GQD structural unit as well as the complexes. (b) Schematic of the electrostatic interactions between the QP and GQD. (c) N 1s XPS spectra of the QP and QP + GQD membranes. (d–g) Phase images of the QP, QP + GQD-1%, QP + GQD-5%, and QP + GQD-10 % membranes by AFM in tapping mode. (h) Count maps of the QP, QP + GQD-1%, QP + GQD-5%, and QP + GQD-10 % membranes by AFM in tapping mode. (i) The SAXS profiles of the QP, QP + GQD-1%, QP + GQD-5%, and QP + GQD-10 % membranes.

(a) The membrane surface potentials and surface charge density of the QP and QP + GQD membranes. (b) Zeta potentials of the QP + GQD-5% membrane as a function of pH values. (c) Ion conductivities of the QP and QP + GQD membranes versus NaCl and Na2SO4 solution at the concentration of 0.1 mol L−1. (d) Schematic and calculation formulas of the SEI model in DLVO interaction calculation. U(D) is the interaction energy. D is the closest distance between hydrated ions and the membrane's surface, a is the radius of the hydrated ion and h is the perpendicular distance between the arc of the hydrated ion (differential surface area rdrdθ) and the point on the surface of the membrane directly below it. E(h) is the interaction energy per unit area between the ion and a membrane surface separated by a distance h, including the DLVO interaction (EDLVO), van der Waals interaction (EVDW), and electrostatic interaction (EEDL). AH is the Hamaker constant, εε0 is permittivity, ѱs is the surface potential of hydrated ions, ѱm is the surface potential of the membrane, and κ is the inverse Debye length. R is the gas constant, F is the Faraday constant, T is absolute temperature, q is the charge of the ion, and r is the hydration radius of hydrated ions. (e) The DLVO interaction energy variation histograms of QP and QP + GQD membranes for Cl− and SO4 2− at separation distance (0.05 nm). (f) Schematic illustration of the shielding positive charge effect to increase Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity as ions enter the membrane.

(a) Schematic diagram of the electrodialysis process for assessing ion separation performance. (b) The Cl− transport rate and selectivity of the QP and QP + GQD membranes in a mixed system of 0.1 mol L−1 NaCl/Na2SO4 at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. (c) Operational stability of QP + GQD-5% membrane in 0.1 mol L−1 NaCl/Na2SO4 mixed system for 20 repetitive cycles. (d) Effect of different pH conditions on Cl− transport rate and selectivity of the QP + GQD-5% membrane. (e) Comparison of the Cl− transport rate and selectivity of the QP + GQD-5% membrane with previously reported ion membranes. (f) The OH− transport rate and selectivity of the QP and QP + GQD membranes in a mixed system of 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH/0.1 mol L−1 Na2WO4 at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. (g) Monovalent anion transport rate and selectivity of the QP + GQD-5% membrane in mixed solutions consisting of different monovalent anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −) and SO4 2− ions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anion exchange membranes (AEMs) that enable effective mono-/divalent anion separation are highly desired for many environment-related applications. Conventional AEMs are characterized with microphase-separated microstructures and rely on the electrostatic attraction between anions and positively charged elements within hydrophilic regions to selectively transport anions. Sufficient charged groups are usually required to ensure high anion permeability, but the stronger interaction encountered by divalent anions than that by monovalent anions results in very low selectivity. We here propose a shielding effect-based strategy, that is, simply mixing piperidinium-based AEMs with carboxylated graphene quantum dots (GQD). These negatively charged GQD can electrostatically interact with the polymer matrix, thereby enhancing the connectivity of hydrophilic regions and also leading to an obvious shielding effect which causes a larger decrease in electrostatic attraction for divalent anions than that for monovalent anions. With the introduction of GQD, the resulting AEMs show obvious enhancement in both anion permeability and anion selectivity, with a Cl− transport rate of 3.2 mol m−2 h−1 and a Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity of 21.8 (8 times of the commercial ACS, 5 times of the original AEMs without GQD), and exceed other reported microphase-separated polymer membranes. This work offers a simple and promising strategy for high-performance ion-selective membranes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymeric anion exchange membranes (AEMs) capable of effective mono-/divalent anion separation are essential to a wide range of environment-related applications, such as brine refining in the chlor-alkali industry [1,2], wastewater and brine resourcing [3,4], and acid/base recovery [5,6]. AEMs rely on their ion channels to selectively conduct anions, and the key issue on high-performance AEMs is how to construct and tune ion transfer channels [7].

The design concept of most AEMs comes from the microphase-separated paradigm (typically represented by Nafion), and their hydrophilic regions consisting of charged functionality serve as the ion channels [8,9]. The ion transport through these membranes relies on the collective interplay of channel structure and electrostatic attraction of charged groups fixed in the ion channels [10]. As such, a sufficient amount of charged groups and wide and continuous hydrophilic phases are usually required to ensure high ion permeability [7,11]. For this purpose, related studies focus on enhancing the self-assembly of charged groups into well-defined microphase separation morphologies within membranes via varied polymer architectures, such as side-chain type, densely functionalized type, etc. [12,13]. Secondary interactions (H-bonds, cation-dipoles, and π-π, etc.) have also been employed as an additional force to further promote the aggregation of charged groups [14–16]. It should be pointed out that divalent anions usually undergo stronger electrostatic interaction with charged groups within ion channels than monovalent anions [17,18]. This fact seems to be disadvantageous to the mono-/divalent ion selectivity. In other words, distinct hydrophilic channels made up by charged groups are always needed to guarantee efficient ion transfer, but may simultaneously cause low ion selectivity, thereby leading to permeability-selectivity tradeoff encountered by current microphase-separated AEMs.

In this work, we propose a shielding effect-based strategy to concurrently realize high anion selectivity and high permeability, that is, simply mixing conventional AEMs with negatively charged nanomaterials that can not only promote the assembly of charged backbone into microphase-separated structures but also weaken the positive electric field to largely decrease the attraction of membrane channels to divalent anions (Fig. 1 a). We exemplify this strategy by using ether-free aryl piperidinium cationic polymers as the matrix and the carboxylated graphene quantum dots (GQD) of ∼1.8 nm in size as the fillers. We find that the electrostatic interaction between these GQD and polymer matrix can enhance the connectivity of hydrophilic regions and also leads to an obvious shielding effect which causes a larger decrease in electrostatic attraction for divalent anions than that for monovalent anions. As such, the membranes can surpass the performance limitation by traditional polymer membranes and show both a high Cl− transport rate of 3.2 mol m−2 h−1 and a Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity of 21.8 (8 times of ACS, the commercial membranes specialized for mono-/divalent anion separation).

Biphenyl (99 %), N-methyl-4-piperidone (98 %), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5 %), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 99 %), trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TFSA, 99 %), dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5 %), 1,6-dibromohexane (98 %), N-methylpiperidine (98 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, M.W. 600, 99 %), chloroform-d (CDCl3, 99.8 %), deuterium oxide (D2O, 99.8 %), dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO‑d 6, 99.8 %) were purchased from Energy Chemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sinopharm Chemical Co. Ltd supplied sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38 %), ethyl acetate (EA, 99.5 %), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.8 %), sodium fluoride (NaF, 99 %), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99 %), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99 %), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.8 %), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8 %), potassium chromate (K2CrO4, 99.5 %), titanium trichloride (TiCl3), potassium thiocyanate (KSCN, 98.5 %) and sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2H2O, 99 %). Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), sulfamic acid (NH2SO3H, 98 %) and poly(acrylic acid) (PAA, M.W. 5000, 50 %) are provided by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Commercial cation exchange membranes (CMX) and anion exchange membranes (ACS) were purchased from ASTOM, Tokuyama Soda, Japan. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken on an LX10-8813 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 in the ATR mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) under the binding energy range of 0–1400 eV using an Al Kα-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV). The H nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H NMR) of the monomers and polymers were recorded on a VNMRS600 instrument (Agilent, USA). CDCl3 and DMSO‑d 6 were used as solvents and tetramethylsilane (TMS) was used as an internal standard. The membrane surface and cross-sectional morphologies were observed by an SU8020 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). The element distributions of membranes were mapped by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) on SEM. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured on a JEM-2100 plus microscope (Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. To improve the contrast of the HRTEM images, the samples were stained with tungstate ions by ion exchange of bromide ions by immersing in a 1.0 mol L−1 Na2WO4 aqueous solution for 48 h and washed with deionized water for 48 h, respectively. After drying at 60 °C, the stained samples were embedded in an epoxy resin, sectioned to 60–70 nm thickness, and then placed on copper grids. The atomic force microscope (AFM) images were performed on a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany) in the tapping mode. The small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) pattern was recorded on an Anton Paar SAXS point 2.0 instrument with a wavelength of 1.5418 Å. The zeta potentials were measured using a Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, England) at room temperature. The membrane surface charging properties were measured as a function of the pH of the electrolyte using a surpass streaming potential analyzer (Surpass 3, Anton Paar, Austria). 1.0 mmol L−1 KCl aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte and 0.1 mol L−1 HCl and 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solutions were used to adjust the pH of the electrolyte. The mechanical properties of the membranes were evaluated using a CMT-4000 electronic testing machine (SANS, China). All the specimens were cut into rectangles with an average length of 5.0 cm and a width of 0.5 cm. The strain rate was 2 mm min−1. The concentration of ions (F−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, and SO4 2−) in the solution was analyzed by ion chromatography (IC6000, wayeal, China). The WO4 2− concentration was recorded by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan).

The ether-free aryl polymer backbone PBP was synthesized through superacid catalysis polymerization [19,20]. The synthesis procedure was as follows: biphenyl (2.5 g, 16.2 mmol) was dissolved in DCM (6 mL), before the addition of N-methyl-4-piperidone (2.23 mL, 19.2 mmol). The reaction mixture was cooled to 0 °C using an ice bath. To avoid excessive fuming, the TFA (1.21 mL, 16.3 mmol) was added dropwise via a funnel before the TFSA (14.4 mL, 162.8 mmol). During the polymerization process, the reaction vessel was placed in an ice bath and the mixture was stirred using a magnetic stirrer. The reaction was halted when the viscosity increased to a level that made magnetic stirring impossible after 4–6 h. The extremely viscous mixture was poured slowly into 2 mol L−1 NaOH (500 mL), resulting in the formation of small polymer lumps. The polymer precipitate in the NaOH solution was left stirring overnight and was then repeatedly filtered and washed thoroughly with deionized water until neutral, the pale-yellow polymer was collected and dried at 80 °C for 24 h.

Pip-Br was synthesized by dropping N-methylpiperidine (10 mL) into a 200 mL EA solution with an excess amount of 1,6-dibromohexane (16 mL). The mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 96 h. A white powder precipitate was formed, and filtered, purified with EA, then dried under vacuum at room temperature for 48 h [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of long alkyl side chain piperidinium quaternary ammonium functionalized PBP (QP)
ce:section.ce:para: The QP polymer was prepared via Menshutkin reaction [22]. PBP (1 g, 4 mmol) was completely dissolved in NMP (10.0 mL) in a round-bottom flask, and the mixture was heated to 80 °C. Then, Pip-Br (0.21 g, 0.6 mmol) was introduced and stirred for 4 days at 80 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown solution was poured into EA to obtain the product. The precipitate was washed with water to remove residual Pip-Br and filtered. Finally, the product was placed in an oven at 60 °C for overnight drying.

Take QP + GQD-5% membrane as an example, 1.0 g QP powder was dissolved in 10 mL NMP at 80 °C, after stirring for 0.5 h. Subsequently, 100 μL of a GQD aqueous solution (0.5 g mL−1) was added. After stirring for an additional 2 h, the mixed solution was poured on smooth and flat glass panels. After drying at 60 °C for 12 h, the glass panels were soaked in deionized water to peel the membrane. The peeled membranes were stored in deionized water. Other composite membranes with varying GQD content were prepared in the same way with exclusively different ratios of the GQD. The QP membrane was prepared in the same way without GQD.

The membranes’ anion transport rate and selectivity were evaluated using an electrodialysis cell with four compartments separated by two commercial CMX membranes and one prepared membrane [23]. The effective membrane area was 7.07 cm2. The diluent chamber was filled with a binary mixture containing 100 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaCl/Na2SO4, 0.1 mol L−1 NaBr/Na2SO4, 0.1 mol L−1 NaNO3/Na2SO4, 0.1 mol L−1 NaF/Na2SO4, or 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH/0.1 mol L−1 Na2WO4. 100 mL of 0.01 mol L−1 NaCl, 0.01 mol L−1 NaBr, 0.01 mol L−1 NaNO3, 0.01 mol L−1 NaF, or 0.01 mol L−1 NaOH were injected into the concentrated chamber. The electrode compartments were filled with 150 mL of 0.3 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution. The solution in each compartment was circulated through a peristaltic pump at a flow rate of 50 mL min−1. The tests were operated at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Samples were collected from the concentrated compartment. The concentration of the OH− and WO4 2− ions was quantified by base-acid titration and the thiocyanate spectrophotometric means, respectively. The concentrations of the remaining various anions on the concentrated side were determined by ion chromatography (IC6000, Wayeal, China). The anion transport rate through the membrane and selectivity were determined by the change in concentration of anions in the concentration chamber after the electrodialysis process for 1 h. To ensure data reproducibility, three parallel tests were performed. In addition, the separation performance of the membranes was evaluated by varying the pH of the test solution to modulate the charge characteristics within the membrane. The pH was adjusted with 0.01 mol L−1 HCl and 0.01 mol L−1 NaOH. To evaluate the ion transport rate and selectivity of the membrane in the five-component system, the experimental procedure was conducted using the same method as in the binary system. The only difference was that the diluent chamber was filled with a mixture of five components: 0.1 mol L−1 NaF, 0.1 mol L−1 NaCl, 0.1 mol L−1 NaBr, 0.1 mol L−1 NaNO3, and 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4, while the concentration chamber was filled with a 0.01 mol L−1 NaCl solution.

All the membranes were placed in the test solutions for 12 h to reach equilibrium. The anion transport rate (J, mol m−2 h−1) can be calculated according to the following equation [24,25]:

To gain insight into the electrostatic interactions between the GQD and QP, we quantified the electrostatic potential (ESP) between them by selecting small molecule models (benzoic acid and 1,1-dimethylpiperidine) as well as polymer QP and GQD using the density functional theory (DFT). The quantum mechanical calculations were carried out by the Gaussian 16 suite program [27]. The DFT calculations were performed with the M062X hybrid functional. The split-valence 6–311++G∗∗ basis set was employed. All the optimized structures were post-processed using Multiwfn2 [28] to obtain their ESP profiles.

The membrane surface charge density (σ, mC m−2) is calculated from the membrane zeta potential using the Gouy-Chapman formula [29,30], as shown below:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the derjaguin-landau-verwey-overbeek (DLVO) interaction energy
ce:section.ce:para:
The DLVO interaction energy between ions and the membrane's surface is calculated using the surface elemental integration (SEI) technique [31,32]. The SEI technique takes into account the total interaction energy between the hydrated ions and the planar membrane surface by integrating the interaction energy per unit area. For the convenience of describing the mathematical formulas, the analysis presented here uses a cylindrical coordinate system, and the expression for the interaction energy is converted to:

E(h) is the interaction energy per unit area between an ion and a membrane surface separated by a distance h, including the DLVO interaction (EDLVO), van der Waals interaction (EVDW), and electrostatic interaction (EEDL), the formula is as follows:

The r is the hydration radius of hydrated ions (which equals 0.332 nm for Cl− and 0.379 nm for SO4 2− [33]).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular design and characterizations of the QP + GQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: GQD belongs to the member of the carbon nanomaterial family, which is zero-dimensional and with excellent dispersibility and richer active sites [34,35]. We here chose the one-step pyrolysis of the citric acid method [36] to synthesize the GQD with a very high mol of carboxyl group per gram (11.4 mmol g−1) (Figs. S1 and S2). HRTEM observations of the size and morphology of the GQD show that the GQD are well dispersed with an average diameter of about 1.8 nm (Fig. 1b and Fig. S3).

We synthesize the side-chain type QP with the superacid-catalyzed polycondensation and the subsequent quaternization (Fig. S4), and then make the composite membranes (named as QP + GQD-x, x (x = 1 %, 5 %, or 10 %) refers to the mass ratio of the GQD to the QP matrix) simply with a solution casting of the solution containing QP and the soluble GQD (Fig. S7). This simple method enables large-area fabrication of mixed matrix membranes (a demonstration with 900 cm2 is shown in Fig. 1c). As demonstrated in Fig. 1d, the resulting membranes show good mechanical properties. The tensile stress and elongation at the break of the QP + GQD membranes are improved compared to the QP membrane, attributed to electrostatic interactions between carboxyl groups on GQD and cationic piperidinium groups on the QP polymer [37]. However, the potential aggregation or poor dispersion of GQD in the polymer matrix at a content of 10 % GQD leads to a decrease in elongation at the break.

The chemical structures of the resultant membranes were first confirmed by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1e). The QP + GQD membrane spectrum shows bands corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the C=O (1700-1750 cm−1) from GQD [38,39] and the bending vibration of C–N+ (982 cm−1) from QP [40,41], indicating the successful introduction of GQD into QP. The successful introduction is further confirmed by XPS spectra (Fig. S8). The C 1s spectra of GQD can be divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV for C–C/CC, 286.4 eV for C–O, and 288.2 eV for O–CO, respectively [42]. In the C 1s spectra of the QP membrane, the two peaks at 284.8 and 285.6 eV represented C–C/CC and C–N of the piperidine ring, respectively [43]. Compared with the QP membrane, the QP + GQD membrane shows two new peaks C–O at 286.4 eV and O–CO at 288.5 eV in addition to two similar C–C/C=C and C–N peaks. Moreover, the increase in the assigned O–C=O binding energy from 288.2 to 288.5 eV peaks in QP + GQD compared to GQD, possibly due to the electrostatic interactions of the carboxyl groups with the piperidinium cation groups.

To illustrate the good dispersion of GQD within the QP matrix, we examined the surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membranes by SEM (Figs. S9 and S10, Figs. 1f and g). The membranes show dense and smooth morphologies and no pinholes or aggregates are observed, suggesting a high level of compatibility between the GQD and the polymer matrix. As shown in Fig. S10, the thicknesses of QP, QP + GQD-1%, QP + GQD-5%, and QP + GQD-10 % membranes are 82, 82, 83, and 85 μm, respectively. It can be found that the thickness of the QP + GQD membrane is almost unchanged compared with that of the QP membrane, tentatively suggesting the thickness effect on the performance of the ion separations can be ruled out. The evenly distributed Na elements from GQD as shown by the EDS of the surface and cross-section of the QP + GQD-5% membrane provide additional evidence for the good dispersion of GQD within the membranes (Figs. 1f and g). In addition, we evaluate the average roughness (Ra) of QP + GQD membranes by AFM. All the membranes show almost unchanged Ra, also reflecting the excellent compatibility between GQD and the QP matrix (Fig. S11) [44,45]. However, it is crucial to note that the electrostatic interactions of the fillers and the QP matrix should not be excessively strong. Initially, sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQD) were incorporated into the QP matrix. However, the complete dissociation of the negatively charged sulfonic acid groups results in overly strong electrostatic interactions between the fillers and the polymer, leading to flocculation during the blending process (Fig. S13). To achieve a homogeneous casting solution and enable the fabrication of high-performance membranes, GQD was chosen as an alternative to SGQD as the filler for producing mixed matrix membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement in the connectivity of ion channels by introducing GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The good dispersion of GQD within membranes is most likely due to the interaction between the carboxylated groups of GQD and the positively charged moieties of QP. To confirm it, we first calculated the electrostatic potential (ESP) distributions of the two charged groups (the piperidinium structural unit and GQD structural unit) and the complex of these two and observed a clear asymmetric charge distribution, which leads to an electrostatic interaction energy between these two of −392.64 kJ mol−1 (Fig. 2 a). The electrostatic interaction energy between QP and GQD was then calculated as a much lower value of −1171.89 kJ mol−1 (Fig. 2b). The electrostatic interactions between the piperidinium structural unit and the GQD structural unit are also verified by the zeta potentials. The values for the small molecule model Pip-Br are positive, and present a downward trend as the GQD (its zeta potential is negative) was added (Fig. S14). In addition, the N 1s XPS spectrum reveals a lower binding energy (402.2 eV) of the C–N+ peak in the QP+GQD membrane compared with that of the QP membrane (Fig. 2c), further confirming this electrostatic interaction [46,47].

The electrostatic interactions between the introduced GQD and the QP may serve as an additional driving force to induce the aggregation of side-chain cationic piperidinium groups in the hydrophilic region, thus improving the connectivity of hydrophilic regions. To verify it, we observed the microstructures of the QP + GQD membranes using microscopic analyses (AFM and HRTEM) [48]. The brighter and darker regions in AFM and HRTEM images are usually considered as the hydrophobic and hydrophilic domains in the QP and QP + GQD membranes, respectively [48,49] (Figs. 2d–g, and Fig. S15). In the tapping mode AFM phase image of the QP membrane, the ionic clusters are discretely distributed in the polymer matrix and isolated from each other, suggesting poor connectivity of the ionic clusters [19]. As the amount of added GQD increases, the hydrophilic regions become more interconnected and maintain sizes of around 12.00–12.35 nm in diameter, but show a larger size (d = 14.25 nm) when the weight ratio of GQD is raised to 10 % (Fig. 2h). This variation tendency was also observed by the results obtained from HRTEM (Fig. S15).

The nanoscale phase separation geometry can also be quantitatively analyzed by the SAXS profiles (Fig. 2i). Distinct scattering intensity peaks are observed for the QP + GQD membranes, which are identified as the ion domains with the aggregation of ionic groups [50,51]. Specifically, the q values of ionomer peaks range from 0.44 to 0.50 nm−1, and the ionic domain size (d) calculated from the Bragg equation (d = 2π/q) ranges from 12.56 to 14.27 nm, which agree with the results from the AFM and HRTEM. By contrast, the SAXS profile of the QP membrane shows no scattering ionomer peak, suggesting that the poor ion aggregation in the QP membrane may be due to the random and dispersive distribution of ion clusters [19]. Therefore, the introduction of GQD to induce aggregation of hydrophilic segments is essential for the formation of a well-defined microphase separation structure in the QP + GQD membrane.

To further elucidate what factors play irreplaceable roles in the formation of microphase-separated structures, we introduce two control samples as the fillers: one is amino graphene quantum dots (NGQD) without any negatively charged groups that can interact with QP (Figs. S16–18), and the other is long-chain polyacrylic acid (PAA) with much-larger size than GQD. No obvious enhancement in microphase-separated morphologies was observed for the mixed matrix membranes using NGQD or PAA as the fillers (Figs. S18a and S18d), indicating that both the electrostatic attraction and the flexibility conferred by GQD's sub-2 nm size contribute to the formation of optimized microstructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement in mono-/divalent anion separation by shielding effect
ce:section.ce:para: Our above results indicate that the introduction of GQD facilitates the formation of microstructures by the interaction between GQD and QP but not by the increase in the ion exchange capacity (IEC). Actually, the insertion of hydrophilic GQD would decrease the IEC because (i) the positively charged piperidinium cation groups have been partially neutralized by the negatively charged carboxyl groups through electrostatic interactions, (ii) the introduced GQD dilutes the density of piperidinium cation groups in the polymer matrix (Fig. 3 a). Interestingly, we observe that this insert raises the number of hydrophilic elements of the resulting membranes but slightly changes the water uptake (WU) and swelling ratio (SR). All the QP and QP + GQD membranes show a WU of below 20 % and an SR of below 10 % (Fig. S19). This little change is most likely due to the enhanced interchain interaction that prevents membrane swelling to some extent.

Enhanced microphase-separated morphologies may be beneficial to selective ion transport [52]. We first use the current-voltage (I–V) curves to determine the limiting current density which is an important factor for ion separation by electrodialysis [53]. All the I–V curves show the typical “S” shape and consist of three different regions: ohmic region (counter-ion transport), plateau region (concentration polarization), and over-limiting region (water splitting) (Figs. S20a and S20b). The limiting current densities can be estimated from the tangent intersection of the I–V curves of the ohmic and plateau regions and are all found to be greater than 20 mA cm−2. Notably, the limiting current density increases with the increase of the GQD content. This improvement comes from the enhancement of the interconnectivity of ion channels which is conducive to ion transport as confirmed by the decreased area resistance of membranes. With the weight ratios of GQD increasing from 0 to 10 %, the area resistances of the corresponding membranes decrease from 3.3 to 1.6 Ω cm2 (Fig. S20c) (all values are much lower than that of the commercial ACS membrane, 4.3 Ω cm2 [5]).

We then continue to employ the I–V profiles obtained with a narrower current range to measure the anion transference numbers (reflecting the selectivity of anion over cation) (Fig. S21) [54]. The Cl− ion transference numbers of the QP and QP + GQD membranes range from 0.87 to 0.92, indicating that these membranes preferentially conduct anions. It should be noted that the increase in the amount of GQD reduces the transference numbers. This is mainly attributed to the presence of carboxyl groups binding with the positively charged groups which decrease both the electrostatic attraction of membranes to anions and the electrostatic repulsion of membranes to anions (associated with the shielding effect).

To further reflect the shielding effect, we detect the change of surface zeta potential of membranes with and without GQD. With the introduction of GQD that electrostatically interacts with the positively charged groups of QP, the surface zeta potentials decrease obviously from 10.45 mV to 0.92 mV, and the surface charge densities correspondingly are reduced from +2.43 mC m−2 to only +0.21 mC m−2 (Fig. 3a). In addition, the surface zeta potentials of membranes are also affected by the pH values due to the protonation of the N element on the uncharged piperidine ring or deprotonation of the carboxyl group in aqueous solution at different pH values, as demonstrated with the QP + GQD-5% membrane exhibiting surface zeta potential decreasing from +4.9 mV to −3.6 mV as the pH rises between pH = 1 and 13 (Fig. 3b).

The shielding effect in this work is expected to benefit the mono-/divalent anion separation. To confirm it, we recorded the I–V curves of membranes in 0.1 mol L−1 of NaCl or Na2SO4 to compare the corresponding membrane conductivities (Fig. S22 and Fig. 3c). Interestingly, the conductivities of the monovalent anion Cl− increase from 0.16 S m−1 for QP to 0.44 S m−1 for QP + GQD-10 %, but the values for SO4 2− do not show a rising trend. Especially to deserve to be mentioned, QP + GQD-5% membrane shows a conductivity for Cl− 23.90 times of that for SO4 2−, while the value for the QP membrane is only 4.97. We speculate that this observation is derived from the shielding effects (the presence of GQD would shield the positive electric field of positively charged groups of mixed matrix membranes), and our speculation is based on the larger decrease in the electrostatic attraction between membranes and divalent anions than that between membranes and monovalent anions (this may increase the transmembrane transport energy barrier for divalent anions).

To confirm our speculation, we then investigated the underlying ion transport mechanism with the aid of theoretical arithmetic. The interaction energies between the ion and the surface of the QP and QP + GQD membranes were quantified using the DLVO theory [31,32,55], which involves the calculation of van der Waals and electrostatic double-layer interaction energies (Fig. 3d and Fig. S23). We compare the DLVO interaction energy curves of the QP and QP + GQD membranes in attracting the divalent SO4 2− and monovalent Cl− (Fig. S23), and find that the interaction energies of QP + GQD membranes are lower than those of the QP membranes for Cl− and SO4 2−. Importantly, the difference in the decrease in membrane attraction to SO4 2− with increasing GQD contents is significantly greater than the difference in the decrease in membrane attraction to Cl−, suggesting that the energy barrier for SO4 2− to enter the membrane is stronger than that for Cl− (Fig. 3e and Fig. S24). Specifically, the difference (ΔE2) between the interaction energy of the QP membrane with SO4 2− and that of the QP + GQD-5% membrane with SO4 2− is 7.76 kT at D = 0.05 nm, which is almost two times as much as the difference in the interaction energy of Cl− (ΔE1 = 3.43 kT), suggesting that the attraction energy for SO4 2− to enter the membrane decreases more than that of Cl− to achieve the ions separation. In summary, the entry of SO4 2− into the QP + GQD-5% membrane is severely impeded, while Cl− experiences only slight entering hindrance, facilitating mono-/divalent anion separation (Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion separation performances of the QP + GQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The above results imply the great potential of QP + GQD membranes as mono-/divalent anion separators and thus encourage us to evaluate their actual separation performance with the electrodialysis process (Fig. 4 a). All the QP + GQD membranes exhibit better separation performance than the QP membrane, as reflected in the higher Cl− transport rate and higher Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity. For example, the QP + GQD-5% membrane shows a Cl− transport rate of 3.2 mol m−2 h−1 (an increment of 18.5 % that of the QP membrane) and a Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity of 21.8 (5 times that of the QP membrane) (Fig. 4b). When the weight ratio of GQD is increased to 10 %, the selectivity of the QP + GQD-10 % membrane decreases due to a slight increase in membrane swelling and a larger size of the hydrophilic zone compared to the QP + GQD-5% membrane (Fig. S19 and Figs. 2g–i). Moreover, this membrane also demonstrates good long-term operational stability as revealed by the almost unchanged values of both Cl− transport rate and Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity during the 20 repetitive cycles of operation (Fig. 4c). We also investigate the influence of solution pH on membrane performance, and find that the separation performance degrade gradually as the pH drops from 11 to 3 (Fig. 4d). This is due to the weakened shielding effects as the carboxylated groups tend to convert into carboxylic acid during this pH regulation. To compare the separation performance between the QP + GQD membrane and other typical polymer membranes, we plot Cl− transport rate versus Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity and observe that the data for the QP + GQD membrane are located at the advantageous region (Fig. 4e and Table S1).

Besides the Cl−/SO4 2− system, the QP + GQD-5% membrane shows superiority in OH−/WO4 2− separation (also known as the base recovery which is widely used in the tungsten ores smelting industry [56]). 1.4 times enhancement in OH− transport rate (5.6 mol m−2 h−1) and 3 times enhancement in OH−/WO4 2− selectivity (259) are observed as GQD with a weight ratio of 5 % are added into the QP matrix (Fig. 4f). Furthermore, poly(aryl piperidinium) based membranes are famous for their outstanding stability in alkali solution [57], and thus the QP + GQD-5% membrane is expected to enable stable long-term operation of base recovery which is confirmed by our cycling experiments (Fig. S25). In addition, characterization of the QP + GQD-5% membrane after long-term operation of alkali recovery by FTIR reveals that the CO absorption peak still exists at 1700-1750 cm−1 (Fig. S26), indicating the stable presence of GQD in the membranes.

The high separation performance for the QP + GQD-5% membrane is also observed in other anion systems (e.g., F−/SO4 2−, Br−/SO4 2−, and NO3 −/SO4 2−) as illustrated in Fig. 4g. Due to the different Gibbs hydration energies and hydration radii of NO3 −, Br−, F−, Cl−, and SO4 2−, the selectivity decreases with the decrease of the difference in Gibbs hydration energies (Table S2) [58]. The selectivity of the ACS membrane for NO3 −/SO4 2−, Br−/SO4 2−, and F−/SO4 2− is notably lower than that of the QP + GQD-5% membrane, with selectivity values of 3.5, 3.3, and 2.5, respectively (Fig. S27). Furthermore, the QP + GQD-5% membrane demonstrates efficient separation of mono-/divalent anion in the complex quintuple system comprising F−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, and SO4 2−. The transport rates of NO3 −, Br−, Cl−, and F− are 2.1, 1.8, 1.6, and 0.5 mol m−2 h−1, respectively (Fig. S28). Although the ion transport rates in the five-component system are lower than those observed in the binary system, likely due to competitive ion transport, the membrane still effectively discriminates mono-/divalent anions. The selectivity of NO3 −, Br−, Cl−, and F− relative to SO4 2− are 34.0, 32.4, 26.8, and 8.2, respectively (Fig. S28). These results highlight the superior performance of the QP + GQD membranes in the selective separation of mixed mono-/divalent anion systems, demonstrating their broader applicability in anion separation processes.

A series of GQD-mixed AEMs are fabricated simply through blending the carboxylated GQD with the side-chain type poly(aryl piperidinium) AEMs. The introduction of GQD of size below 2 nm can electrostatically interact with the polymer matrix (QP), thus promoting the aggregation of charged groups of QP to enhance the formation of interconnected ion channels beneficial for ion conduction and simultaneously modulating the microchemical environment around these positively charged groups to induce strong shielding effect. This effect leads to a larger decrease in the electrostatic attraction between membranes and divalent anions than that between membranes and monovalent anions. The larger decrease of electrostatic attraction results in a larger transmembrane transport energy barrier for divalent anions, thereby resulting in a high selectivity of monovalent anions over divalent anions. The GQD-mixed AEMs obviously exceed other polymer membranes concerning the Cl−/SO4 2− separation, and present a Cl− transport rate of 3.2 mol m−2 h−1 (an increment of 52.3 % compared with that of the commercial ACS membrane) and a Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity of 21.8 (8 times that of the commercial ACS membrane). This work provides a simple and feasible strategy for the development of monovalent anion-selective membranes.
